title: Focus on context: disease and demography
description: Read passages and check your understanding of the Black Death.
topic_path: Focus on context: disease and demography

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following excerpt and answer the questions that follow. Pay attention to how the historical context of trade and military interaction affected how plague spread. 

>The Tartars [Mongols of the Golden Horde] set on [attacked] a Genoese trading station in the city of Tana and chased the merchants to their redoubt [fort] at Caffa… a town on the Crimean coast which the Genoese had built and fortified as a base from which to trade with the [East]...Their plans were disastrously disturbed by the plague which was soon taking heavy toll of the besiegers…[F]ew places are so vulnerable to disease as a besieged city and it was not long before the plague was as active within the city as without… [The Genoese] took to their galleys and fled from the Black Sea towards the Mediterranean. With them travelled the plague. 
 
One of the main trade routes by which the spices and silks from the East reached the European market was by way of Baghdad and then along the Tigris [River] and through Armenia to the entrepot [trading] stations of the Italian merchants in the Crimea. Nothing is more likely than that the plague should travel with the great caravans and spread itself among the [Mongols] of the Crimea. 

From: Philip Ziegler, _The Black Death_ (New York: Harper Perennial, 2009, 1969), 15-16.

**How did the plague most likely travel from the Far East to the Mongols in the Crimea, according to Ziegler?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Through caravan routes that crossed central Asia.
Through Genoese traders who carried the plague on their ships.
Through military campaigning.
Through migrating animals.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following excerpt and answer the questions that follow. Pay attention to how the historical context of trade and military interaction affected how plague spread. 

>The Tartars [Mongols of the Golden Horde] set on [attacked] a Genoese trading station in the city of Tana and chased the merchants to their redoubt [fort] at Caffa… a town on the Crimean coast which the Genoese had built and fortified as a base from which to trade with the [East]...Their plans were disastrously disturbed by the plague which was soon taking heavy toll of the besiegers…[F]ew places are so vulnerable to disease as a besieged city and it was not long before the plague was as active within the city as without… [The Genoese] took to their galleys and fled from the Black Sea towards the Mediterranean. With them travelled the plague. 
 
One of the main trade routes by which the spices and silks from the East reached the European market was by way of Baghdad and then along the Tigris [River] and through Armenia to the entrepot [trading] stations of the Italian merchants in the Crimea. Nothing is more likely than that the plague should travel with the great caravans and spread itself among the [Mongols] of the Crimea. 

From: Philip Ziegler, _The Black Death_ (New York: Harper Perennial, 2009, 1969), 15-16.

**Once in Crimea, how did the plague get to Europe?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Through Genoese traders who carried the plague on their ships.
Through overland trading caravans.
Through military campaigning.
Through migrating animals.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the following excerpt and answer the questions that follow. Pay attention to how the historical context of trade and military interaction affected how plague spread. 

>The Tartars [Mongols of the Golden Horde] set on [attacked] a Genoese trading station in the city of Tana and chased the merchants to their redoubt [fort] at Caffa… a town on the Crimean coast which the Genoese had built and fortified as a base from which to trade with the [East]...Their plans were disastrously disturbed by the plague which was soon taking heavy toll of the besiegers…[F]ew places are so vulnerable to disease as a besieged city and it was not long before the plague was as active within the city as without… [The Genoese] took to their galleys and fled from the Black Sea towards the Mediterranean. With them travelled the plague. 
 
One of the main trade routes by which the spices and silks from the East reached the European market was by way of Baghdad and then along the Tigris [River] and through Armenia to the entrepot [trading] stations of the Italian merchants in the Crimea. Nothing is more likely than that the plague should travel with the great caravans and spread itself among the [Mongols] of the Crimea. 

From: Philip Ziegler, _The Black Death_ (New York: Harper Perennial, 2009, 1969), 15-16.

**Which of the following claims connecting can be supported by evidence from this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The plague was able to spread across Asia and Europe largely due to the interconnectedness of trade routes.
The plague spread only through military campaigns, since these concentrated many people in one place.
The Mongol siege of Caffa was the only route by which the plague could have to spread to Europe.
The plague would likely have spread even without highly interconnected trade routes.

assessment_items.question_content:
This excerpt is the opening of the Ordinance of Laborers, passed in 1349 in England. It was a reaction to the large number of deaths caused by the plague. 
 
>[A] great part of the people, and especially of workmen and servants, late died of the pestilence [plague], many seeing the necessity of masters, and great scarcity of servants, will not serve unless they may receive excessive wages, and some rather willing to beg in idleness, than by labor to get their living; we, considering the grievous incommodities [problems], which of the lack especially of ploughmen and such laborers may hereafter come, have upon deliberation and treaty with the prelates and the nobles, and learned men assisting us, of their mutual counsel ordained:
That every man and woman of our realm of England ... able in body, and within the age of threescore years [under 60], [who is not a landowner or engaged in a trade], be required to serve, ... and take only the wages...which were accustomed to be given in … the twentieth year of [King Edward III’s] reign of England [1347], or five or six other common years next before [the previous five or six years]. 

From: Albert Beebe White and Wallace Notestein, eds. Source Problems in English History (New York: Harper and Brothers Publishers, 1915).

**What was the purpose of the Ordinance of Labourers?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To keep wages at pre-plague levels.
To prevent further spread of the plague.
To raise wages in response to population loss.
To improve working conditions.

assessment_items.question_content:
This excerpt is the opening of the Ordinance of Laborers, passed in 1349 in England. It was a reaction to the large number of deaths caused by the plague. 
 
>[A] great part of the people, and especially of workmen and servants, late died of the pestilence [plague], many seeing the necessity of masters, and great scarcity of servants, will not serve unless they may receive excessive wages, and some rather willing to beg in idleness, than by labor to get their living; we, considering the grievous incommodities [problems], which of the lack especially of ploughmen and such laborers may hereafter come, have upon deliberation and treaty with the prelates and the nobles, and learned men assisting us, of their mutual counsel ordained:
That every man and woman of our realm of England ... able in body, and within the age of threescore years [under 60], [who is not a landowner or engaged in a trade], be required to serve, ... and take only the wages...which were accustomed to be given in … the twentieth year of [King Edward III’s] reign of England [1347], or five or six other common years next before [the previous five or six years]. 

From: Albert Beebe White and Wallace Notestein, eds. Source Problems in English History (New York: Harper and Brothers Publishers, 1915).

**What was the historical context that explains why the Ordinance of Labourers passed in 1349, specifically?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The plague that hit England in 1348 killed huge numbers of people, causing a labor shortage.
A new king had taken the throne and changed the laws to demonstrate his power.
Workers were upset with their conditions of employment and demanded to the king that these changes be made.
Large numbers of workers had emigrated from England to avoid the plague.
